items.prompt: What is the main purpose of rejecting infeasible brute force before implementation?

options.text: Avoid spending implementation effort on a growth class that cannot satisfy the stated constraints.
Prove brute force is always conceptually wrong.
Choose shorter variable names.
Replace all reasoning with low-level optimization.

feedback.reason: Constraint analysis should eliminate impossible strategy classes early.
feedback.details: Implementation detail matters only after the asymptotic and output requirements are plausible.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.brute_force_never: Brute force can be appropriate for small inputs or required enumeration.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.micro_optimize: Constant-factor tuning cannot repair an orders-of-magnitude growth mismatch.

items.prompt: Is an O(2^n) subset search automatically unacceptable?

options.text: No. It can be appropriate for small n or when all subsets must be output.
Yes. Exponential algorithms are never usable.
No. It is always acceptable.
It is acceptable only on sorted input.

feedback.reason: Feasibility depends on concrete n, per-state work, and whether enumeration is mandated.
feedback.details: The goal is to reject infeasible brute force, not to ban exhaustive methods categorically.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_bad: For n around 20, the state count can be manageable.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_good: Larger n quickly makes it infeasible.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_sorted: Subset count is unrelated to sort order.

items.prompt: For n = 100,000, why should an O(n²) pair check usually be rejected under ordinary contest-scale budgets?

options.text: It implies about 10 billion pair operations before overhead.
Because any nested loop is forbidden.
Because pairs cannot be represented.
Because both loops use the name n.

feedback.reason: Substituting the maximum constraint exposes a cost far above common abstract budgets.
feedback.details: A different invariant, data structure, or problem reduction is needed unless the output itself is that large.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.nested_syntax: Some nested loops have linear aggregate work.

constraints: Use the dominant count n² and ignore constant factors.

items.prompt: Why is enumerating all n! permutations usually rejected much earlier than enumerating all 2^n subsets?

options.text: Factorial growth overtakes exponential 2^n rapidly.
Permutation count is actually linear.
2^n is always larger than n!.
Permutations require sorting first.

feedback.reason: Factorial branching multiplies by a decreasing sequence n·(n-1)·...·1.
feedback.details: Even moderate n can produce hundreds of millions or more candidate orderings.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.subsets_more: For sufficiently large n, n! is much larger.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_required: Sorting is not the growth explanation.

items.prompt: A branch-and-bound algorithm has a heuristic prune but no proof that it removes branches in the worst case. What worst-case class should still be considered?

options.text: The full brute-force search-tree class.
O(1) because pruning exists.
O(n) because each branch is visited once.
No worst-case analysis is possible.

feedback.reason: A heuristic can improve typical behavior without changing the formal worst case.
feedback.details: Only a guaranteed structural reduction justifies a stronger worst-case bound.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: Some inputs may trigger no useful pruning.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.linear: The number of branches may be exponential.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.average_only: The unpruned tree gives a valid upper bound.

items.prompt: A task asks to list all n! permutations. Should O(n!) enumeration be rejected solely because it is factorial?

options.text: No. The output contract itself requires n! results, so factorial output work is unavoidable.
Yes. The requested output should be ignored.
A constant-time algorithm can return all results symbolically under the same materialized-output contract.
The best possible time is always O(n²).

feedback.reason: Avoidable brute force must be separated from mandatory enumeration.
feedback.details: The relevant goal becomes low overhead per output and correct output-sensitive accounting.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_always: An algorithm cannot return all permutations without producing them.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_n2: That is smaller than the output size.

items.prompt: An O(n³) algorithm is proposed for n = 400 under an abstract 100,000,000-operation budget. What first-pass conclusion follows?

options.text: 400³ = 64 million, so it is provisionally plausible before constants and memory review.
Reject every cubic algorithm regardless of n.
The count is about 64 quadrillion.
The count is constant because 400 is fixed in the statement.

feedback.reason: A higher-order polynomial can be feasible at a small enough maximum n.
feedback.details: The estimate is close enough to require margin and operation-cost review, not automatic acceptance.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: The feasibility check substitutes a maximum, but the algorithmic class remains cubic.

constraints: Use an abstract budget of 100,000,000 equal-cost operations and ignore constants for the first pass.

items.prompt: A proposed search checks every pair of n values, but the task must return every qualifying pair and k can be Θ(n²). What is the correct review?

options.text: Quadratic work may be unavoidable in worst cases because the required output can itself be quadratic.
Reject O(n²) without considering output.
Returning pairs always uses O(1) output space.
Rename the loops to avoid the quadratic bound.

feedback.reason: An output-sensitive problem can legitimately require a high worst-case bound.
feedback.details: The algorithm should still avoid unnecessary work when k is small, but cannot beat the materialized output lower bound.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_reject: The output lower bound may already be Ω(n²).
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant_output: There can be quadratically many pairs.

items.prompt: Which observation best rejects a full O(3^n) state search for n = 30 under a 100,000,000-state budget?

options.text: 3^30 is about 206 trillion states, far above the budget.
The code must contain exactly three nested loops.
n = 30 makes every algorithm easy.
The states have long names.

feedback.reason: Concrete state-space size supplies the rejection proof.
feedback.details: 3^30 is approximately 205,891,132,094,649 states. The gap is too large for constant-factor tuning to solve.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.three_loops: Exponential state search need not use three loops.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_small: Exponential growth can be enormous at 30.

constraints: Use an abstract budget of 100,000,000 states.

items.prompt: Why is "nested loops always mean brute force" an unreliable heuristic?

options.text: Nested loops may perform bounded aggregate work or implement a necessary structured computation.
Nested loops never increase runtime.
Brute force cannot be written recursively.
Only loop variable names define brute force.

feedback.reason: Brute force is about enumerating a large candidate space without enough structure, not about syntax alone.
feedback.details: Two-pointer, stack, and output-sensitive algorithms can contain nested loops with justified bounds.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.brute_force_no_loops: It can use loops or recursion.

items.prompt: Which signs justify rejecting a candidate algorithm before implementation? Select all that apply.

options.text: Substituted dominant work exceeds the budget by orders of magnitude.
The required table or state set cannot fit the memory limit.
The algorithm explores far more candidates than the required output or decision contract demands.
The technique is unfamiliar to the reviewer.

feedback.reason: Rejection should be based on workload, resources, and contract mismatch.
feedback.details: Subjective familiarity is not evidence.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unfamiliar: Familiarity is not a complexity argument.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.orders_over_budget: Constants cannot bridge a massive gap.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.memory_over_budget: Space alone can reject a design.

items.prompt: Which situations can make exhaustive search reasonable? Select all that apply.

options.text: The maximum n makes the full state space fit with margin.
The contract requires materializing every candidate solution.
A small secondary parameter bounds the exponential part.
The state space is enormous and no pruning or output requirement exists.

feedback.reason: Brute force is a tool with a domain of validity.
feedback.details: The review should identify the parameter that keeps exhaustive work bounded.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.large_unbounded: That is the case to reject.

items.prompt: Which claims are insufficient to improve a brute-force worst-case bound? Select all that apply.

options.text: The implementation usually prunes many branches.
The benchmark machine is fast.
The inner loop is highly optimized.
A proof limits every search to polynomially many states.

feedback.reason: A stronger worst-case claim needs a structural proof, not typical behavior or constants.
feedback.details: Guaranteed state bounds are qualitatively different.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.guaranteed_bound: This is sufficient to change the worst-case class.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.usually_prunes: Worst-case inputs may avoid the heuristic.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.fast_machine: Hardware does not change the growth class.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.micro_optimized: Constant factors do not remove exponential growth.

items.prompt: Which distinctions are needed when reviewing an expensive enumerator? Select all that apply.

options.text: Whether the task asks only for existence/count or for every result.
The maximum number and size of returned results.
Whether the algorithm generates duplicate candidates that the contract does not require.
Whether loop variables are named i and j.

feedback.reason: A brute-force label is meaningful only relative to the requested result contract.
feedback.details: Output and duplicate behavior reveal whether high work is necessary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_names: Names do not affect candidate count.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.decision_vs_list: Enumeration may be avoidable for a decision problem.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates: Duplicate search can be avoidable overhead.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for rejecting an infeasible candidate algorithm.

elements.text: Derive its dominant time and space expressions.
Substitute maximum constraints and output requirements.
Compare estimated work and storage with explicit budgets.
If infeasible, identify exploitable order, reuse, monotonicity, or smaller parameters.

feedback.reason: Rejection should be quantitative and followed by a search for structure.
feedback.details: Implementing first and discovering the growth mismatch later wastes effort.

items.prompt: Arrange the review of an apparently exponential output problem.

elements.text: Determine whether every exponential candidate must be returned.
Estimate the maximum materialized output size.
Compare explored states with required outputs.
Classify the exponential work as unavoidable output cost or avoidable brute force.

feedback.reason: The same asymptotic class can be necessary for enumeration and wasteful for a decision problem.
feedback.details: Output-sensitive analysis prevents rejecting a correct optimal enumerator for the wrong reason.

items.prompt: An algorithm enumerates all subsets for n = 40. Estimate the state count and budget fit for a 100,000,000-state budget.

feedback.reason: The state space exceeds the budget by roughly four orders of magnitude.
feedback.details: This is infeasible unless the actual parameter is smaller or a strong structural reduction exists.

items.prompt: An algorithm enumerates all 11! permutations. Estimate the state count and whether it fits a 100,000,000-state budget before per-permutation work.

feedback.reason: The raw state count fits the abstract budget.
feedback.details: Copying length-11 outputs or expensive checks can still make the real workload tighter.

items.prompt: For n = 100,000, an algorithm compares every unordered pair. Estimate pair count and budget fit for 100,000,000 operations.
constraints: Use n(n - 1)/2 and ignore constant per-pair overhead.

feedback.reason: There are approximately 4,999,950,000 unordered pairs.
feedback.details: The quadratic pair space alone exceeds the stated budget.

items.prompt: A task must return every subset of n = 24 elements, materializing each selected-element list. Select the number of subsets and a tight asymptotic output-volume bound.
constraints: Across the full power set, each element appears in exactly half of the subsets.

feedback.reason: There are 2^n subsets, and total materialized element occurrences are n·2^(n-1).
feedback.details: The exponential output requirement means exponential work is unavoidable under this contract.
